questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Which sentence contains a SIMILE?
options: The sun smiled down on us.
questions.explain: A simile uses 'like' or 'as' to compare two things. 'Her smile was like sunshine' uses 'like,' making it a simile.

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: What does the idiom 'cost an arm and a leg' mean?
questions.explain: 'Cost an arm and a leg' is an idiom that means something is very expensive.

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: 'The wind whispered through the trees.' This phrase is an example of ___.
questions.explain: Personification gives a human action (whispering) to a non-human thing (the wind). Wind cannot actually whisper.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the CORRECT possessive form?
questions.explain: 'Children' is already a plural noun (not ending in -s), so add apostrophe + s: children's.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Choose the sentence with the CORRECT use of commas.
options: I like cats dogs and fish.
I like, cats dogs, and fish.
I like cats, dogs, and fish.
I like cats, dogs and, fish.
questions.explain: Use commas to separate items in a list: cats, dogs, and fish. The comma goes after each item except the last.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which sentence uses CONSISTENT verb tense throughout?
options: Yesterday, she runs to school and arrived on time.
Yesterday, she ran to school and arrives on time.
Yesterday, she runs to school and arrives on time.
Yesterday, she ran to school and arrived on time.
questions.explain: 'Yesterday' signals past tense. Both verbs must be past tense: ran and arrived.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'its' (WITHOUT an apostrophe)?
questions.explain: 'Its' (no apostrophe) shows possession — the dog's paw. 'It's' (with apostrophe) is short for 'it is.'

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Read: 'Bees live in large colonies. Worker bees collect nectar and make honey. Bees also carry pollen from flower to flower, helping plants grow. Without bees, many plants would not survive.' What is the MAIN IDEA?
options: Bees are important insects that make honey and help plants grow.
questions.explain: The main idea covers the whole passage. The others are supporting details. Bees' importance — honey-making AND pollination — is the main idea.

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Read: 'Rain forests are found near the equator. They are home to more than half of the world's species. Rain forests also absorb carbon dioxide and produce oxygen. Sadly, they are being cut down at a fast rate.' What is the BEST main idea?
options: Rain forests are being cut down.
Rain forests are near the equator.
Rain forests are vital ecosystems that support life and help our planet.
Half the world's species live in rain forests.
questions.explain: The best main idea covers all key points in the passage — the vital role of rain forests — not just one detail.

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Read: 'Edison failed over 1,000 times inventing the light bulb. He said, "I've just found 10,000 ways that won't work." His persistence led to more than 1,000 successful inventions.' What LESSON does this passage teach?
options: Light bulbs were hard to invent.
Persistence and hard work lead to success.
questions.explain: The passage focuses on Edison's persistence through failure, teaching that hard work and determination lead to success.

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Read: 'In 1920, women in the U.S. gained the right to vote. Before that, suffragists marched, wrote letters, and gave speeches for years. Their hard work finally paid off with the 19th Amendment.' What is the main idea?
options: Suffragists wrote letters and gave speeches.
Women worked hard for years to earn the right to vote, and they succeeded.
questions.explain: The main idea is about the long struggle and eventual success — not just one action like marching or writing letters.

questions.subject: reading
questions.passage: Rain forests cover only about 6% of Earth's surface, but they are home to more than half of the world's plants and animals. These dense forests stay warm and wet all year because they are near the equator. Rain forests have four main layers: the emergent layer at the top, the canopy, the understory, and the forest floor. Each layer is home to different kinds of life. Rain forests also help our planet by absorbing carbon dioxide and producing oxygen.
questions.prompt: Why do you think the forest floor gets very little sunlight?
options: There are no trees on the forest floor.
The canopy and other layers above block most of the sunlight.
The forest floor is located underground.
The forest floor is always covered in water.
questions.explain: The text describes four layers above the forest floor (emergent, canopy, understory). These dense layers block sunlight from reaching the bottom.

questions.subject: reading
questions.passage: Rain forests cover only about 6% of Earth's surface, but they are home to more than half of the world's plants and animals. These dense forests stay warm and wet all year because they are near the equator. Rain forests have four main layers: the emergent layer at the top, the canopy, the understory, and the forest floor. Each layer is home to different kinds of life. Rain forests also help our planet by absorbing carbon dioxide and producing oxygen.
questions.prompt: What is the BEST main idea of this passage?
options: Rain forests are dense, wet forests that support enormous biodiversity and help the planet.
Rain forests cover 6% of Earth's surface.
Each layer of a rain forest supports different life.
questions.explain: The best main idea wraps up all the key ideas: what rain forests are, where they are, what lives there, and their importance.

questions.subject: reading
questions.passage: Mia had always been afraid of trying new things. When her class signed up for the school talent show, she wanted to say no. But her teacher encouraged her to try. Mia practiced singing every day after school. On the night of the show, her hands shook as she stepped onto the stage. She took a deep breath, remembered all her weeks of practice, and began to sing. The audience clapped and cheered. As she walked off the stage, Mia realized she wasn't afraid anymore.
questions.prompt: Why did Mia decide to practice singing every day?
options: She wanted to impress her friends.
Her teacher told her she was required to practice.
She was determined to be ready for the talent show.
questions.explain: The passage says Mia practiced every day after agreeing to do the show. Her motivation was to be prepared — to give herself the best chance at success.

questions.subject: reading
questions.passage: Mia had always been afraid of trying new things. When her class signed up for the school talent show, she wanted to say no. But her teacher encouraged her to try. Mia practiced singing every day after school. On the night of the show, her hands shook as she stepped onto the stage. She took a deep breath, remembered all her weeks of practice, and began to sing. The audience clapped and cheered. As she walked off the stage, Mia realized she wasn't afraid anymore.
questions.prompt: What is the THEME of this story?
options: School talent shows are fun for everyone.
Facing your fears and working hard can lead to personal growth.
Singing is difficult to learn on your own.
questions.explain: The theme is the big lesson of the story. Mia overcame her fear by working hard, and as a result she grew and changed — this is the theme.

questions.subject: logic
questions.prompt: What number comes next? 2, 6, 18, 54, ___
questions.explain: Each number is multiplied by 3. 54 × 3 = 162.

questions.subject: logic
questions.prompt: Puppy is to dog as kitten is to ___.
questions.explain: A puppy grows into a dog. A kitten grows into a cat. The relationship is 'young animal → adult animal.'

questions.subject: logic
questions.prompt: All plants need water. A cactus is a plant. What conclusion MUST be true?
options: Water is more important for animals.
questions.explain: If all plants need water, and a cactus is a plant, then a cactus must need water. This is logical deduction.

questions.subject: logic
questions.prompt: A number pattern: 100, 91, 82, 73, ___. What is the rule, and what is the next number?

questions.subject: logic
questions.prompt: Red is to stop as green is to ___.
questions.explain: On a traffic light, red means stop and green means go. The relationship is color → action.

questions.subject: logic
questions.prompt: 5 kids stand in line. Dan is 1st. Ava is 2nd. Ben is right behind Ava. Carlos is last. Who is 4th?
questions.explain: Order: Dan (1st), Ava (2nd), Ben (3rd, right behind Ava), Emma (4th), Carlos (5th/last). Emma is 4th.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Mia had 15 stickers. She gave 4 to her sister and bought 6 more. How many stickers does Mia have now?
questions.explain: 15 − 4 = 11, then 11 + 6 = 17 stickers.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: There are 9 kids on the swings and 7 kids on the slide. Then 5 kids go home. How many kids are left?
questions.explain: 9 + 7 = 16 kids total, then 16 − 5 = 11 kids left.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Which number has 4 tens and 7 ones?
questions.explain: 4 tens = 40 and 7 ones = 7, so the number is 47.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Which number is greater: 6 tens 2 ones, or 7 tens 0 ones?
questions.explain: 6 tens 2 ones = 62 and 7 tens 0 ones = 70, and 70 > 62.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: School starts at 8 o'clock. Art class is 1 and a half hours later. What time does art class start?
questions.explain: 8:00 plus 1 hour = 9:00, plus 30 more minutes = 9:30.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Leo ate lunch at 12:00. He played for 30 minutes after lunch. What time did he finish playing?
questions.explain: 12:00 plus 30 minutes = 12:30.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A crayon is 5 cubes long. A pencil is 8 cubes long. A marker is 6 cubes long. Which is shortest?
questions.explain: The crayon is 5 cubes, which is less than 6 (marker) and 8 (pencil), so it is shortest.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A book is 9 paper clips long. A box is 12 paper clips long. How much longer is the box than the book?
questions.explain: 12 − 9 = 3 paper clips longer.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: 14 + ___ = 20. What is the missing number?
questions.explain: 20 − 14 = 6, so the missing addend is 6.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: ___ + 9 = 17. What number goes in the blank?

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Kai has 3 bags with 4 crayons in each bag. Is the total number of crayons even or odd?
options: Odd, because 12 cannot be split evenly
Even, because 12 can be split into pairs
questions.explain: 3 × 4 = 12, and 12 is even because it can be split into 6 pairs with none left over.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: There are 7 fish in one tank and 5 fish in another tank. Is the total even or odd?
options: Even, because 7 and 5 are both odd
Even, because 12 can be split into pairs
questions.explain: 7 + 5 = 12, and 12 is even because it can be grouped into pairs with none left over.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: What are the next two numbers? 5, 10, 15, 20, ___, ___
questions.explain: The pattern skips by 5 each time, so after 20 comes 25, then 30.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Look at the shape pattern: circle, square, triangle, circle, square, triangle. What shape comes 9th?
questions.explain: The pattern repeats every 3 shapes. The 9th shape is at position 9 ÷ 3 = 3rd in the group, which is triangle.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: The word 'bat' can mean two things. Which sentence uses 'bat' to mean an animal?
options: She hit the ball with a bat.
A bat flew out of the cave at night.
The bat broke during the game.
questions.explain: In 'A bat flew out of the cave,' bat refers to the flying animal, not a sports tool.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: What does the compound word 'raincoat' mean?
options: A coat you wear to stay dry in the rain
A coat that is colored like rain
A coat used to make rain
questions.explain: A raincoat is a coat you wear to protect yourself from rain and stay dry.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct?
questions.explain: 'Dogs' is plural, so the correct verb is 'bark' without an -s: 'The dogs bark loudly.'

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: What is the correct plural of 'fox'?
questions.explain: Words ending in -x get -es added to make the plural: fox → foxes.

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Zara was nervous. She held her puppy close as the thunder boomed outside. Why is Zara probably holding her puppy?
options: She wants to show her puppy to a friend.
She is scared and wants to feel safe.
She is trying to keep the puppy warm.
She is playing a game with the puppy.
questions.explain: Because Zara is nervous and the thunder is booming, she is likely scared and holding the puppy to feel safe.

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Omar planted a seed. He watered it every day and put it near the window. Two weeks later, he saw a tiny green sprout. What helped the seed grow?
options: Water and sunlight from the window
Keeping the seed in the dark
Putting the seed in the freezer
questions.explain: Omar watered the seed daily and placed it near sunlight from the window, which helped it grow.

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Jada felt exhausted after running the race. What does exhausted mean?
questions.explain: Exhausted means very tired, which makes sense after running a race.

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Which word in this sentence is a verb? 'The bird sang a pretty song.'
questions.explain: 'Sang' is the verb because it is the action the bird did.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A caterpillar makes a chrysalis. What comes out of the chrysalis next?
questions.explain: After a caterpillar forms a chrysalis, it goes through metamorphosis and emerges as a butterfly.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: In which season would you most likely need an umbrella AND a warm coat at the same time?
questions.explain: In fall or spring, it can be cool and rainy, so you might need both a warm coat and an umbrella.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A soccer ball is rolling toward the goal. The goalie kicks it back. Did the goalie use a push or pull, and what happened to the ball?
options: A pull — the ball sped up in the same direction
A push — the ball changed direction
A pull — the ball stopped moving
questions.explain: A kick is a push force. Pushing the ball changed its direction and sent it back the other way.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Maya has a ribbon 45 cm long. She cuts off 17 cm, then 9 more cm. How many cm of ribbon are left?
questions.explain: 45 − 17 = 28, then 28 − 9 = 19 centimeters remaining.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Luis's desk is 3 feet long. His bookshelf is 2 feet longer than his desk. His bed is 4 feet longer than his bookshelf. How long is his bed?
questions.explain: Desk = 3 ft, bookshelf = 3 + 2 = 5 ft, bed = 5 + 4 = 9 feet.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Aisha's soccer practice starts at 3:15 PM and lasts 45 minutes. What time does it end?
questions.explain: 3:15 PM + 45 minutes = 4:00 PM.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: The clock shows 7:45. Jaylen needs to be at school in 20 minutes. What time does school start?

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Mrs. Kim has 34 stickers to share equally between 2 friends with none left over. Can she do it, and how do you know?
options: Yes, because 34 is even and splits into two equal groups of 17.
No, because 34 is odd and one friend would get more.
Yes, because 34 ends in 4 and all numbers ending in 4 split evenly into 3 groups.
No, because 34 is too large to share equally.
questions.explain: 34 is even (ends in 4), so 34 ÷ 2 = 17 each.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Omar adds 13 + 15. He also adds 12 + 14. Which statement is true about his answers?
options: The sum of the odd numbers is odd; the sum of the evens is even.
The sum of the odd numbers is even; the sum of the evens is odd.
questions.explain: Odd + odd = even (28) and even + even = even (26), so both sums are even.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Which number is the same as 400 + 60 + 3?
questions.explain: 400 + 60 + 3 = 463; the 4 is in the hundreds place, 6 in tens, 3 in ones.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: 752 = 700 + ___ + 2. What is the missing number?
questions.explain: The tens digit is 5, so the missing value is 50.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Look at this number pattern: 5, 10, 20, 40, ___. What is the rule and what is the missing number?
questions.explain: Each number is doubled: 5→10→20→40→80.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A classroom has 4 rows of 6 desks. The teacher adds 1 more row of 6. How many desks total?

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Sofia arranges stickers in 3 rows × 8 each. Her brother uses 4 rows × 6 each. Who has more stickers, and how many more?
questions.explain: Sofia: 3 × 8 = 24. Brother: 4 × 6 = 24. They are equal.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: There are 12 apples in a basket. One-fourth of them are red. How many apples are red?
questions.explain: 1/4 of 12 = 12 ÷ 4 = 3 red apples.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A pizza is cut into 4 equal slices. Mia eats 1 slice and her dad eats 2 slices. What fraction of the pizza is left?
questions.explain: 1 + 2 = 3 slices eaten. 4 − 3 = 1 slice left, which is 1/4.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A bag has some marbles. After adding 15 more there are 43 total. Then 8 are removed. How many marbles are in the bag now?
questions.explain: 43 − 8 = 35 marbles remaining.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Choose the sentence that uses the correct plural of the word 'child.'
options: The childs ran to the playground.
The childrens ran to the playground.
The children ran to the playground.
The childer ran to the playground.
questions.explain: The irregular plural of 'child' is 'children.'

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the WRONG plural form of the noun?
options: Three geese flew over the pond.
The mice hid under the cabinet.
My two foots hurt after the long walk.
The men waited in line at the store.
questions.explain: 'Foots' is incorrect; the irregular plural of 'foot' is 'feet.'

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Marcus __________ his lunch box to school but forgot his homework. Which word correctly completes the sentence?
questions.explain: The irregular past tense of 'bring' is 'brought.'

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Yesterday, Tiana __________ to the library and __________ home five books. Which pair of words correctly completes the sentence?
questions.explain: The irregular past tense of 'go' is 'went' and of 'bring' is 'brought.'

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Read: 'The exhausted puppy curled up in its bed and fell asleep right away after running all afternoon.' What does 'exhausted' most likely mean?
questions.explain: Clues like 'fell asleep right away' and 'running all afternoon' show that exhausted means very tired.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Read: 'Bees fly from flower to flower collecting nectar. They use nectar to make honey. Bees also move pollen from one flower to another.' What is the MAIN IDEA?
options: Bees are important and hardworking insects.
Flowers need bees to make nectar.
questions.explain: The paragraph covers multiple roles bees play, so the main idea is about bees being important and hardworking.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an apostrophe CORRECTLY to show something belongs to more than one girl?
options: The girl's backpacks were by the door.
The girls's backpacks were by the door.
The girls' backpacks were by the door.
The girls backpack's were by the door.
questions.explain: When a plural noun ends in -s, the apostrophe goes AFTER the s (girls') to show possession.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Read: 'The scientist observed the ants carefully, watching every move without disturbing them.' What does 'observed' most likely mean?
questions.explain: The clue 'watching every move' tells us that observed means watched closely.

questions.subject: reading
questions.passage: Leo always walked slowly past the old house on Maple Street. The windows were dark, the gate creaked, and tall weeds had grown over the path. One afternoon, he heard soft music coming from inside.
questions.prompt: Why does Leo most likely walk SLOWLY past the house?
options: He is listening to music he likes.
He is scared of the house because it looks creepy.
He wants to see the flowers in the garden.
He is waiting for his friend to catch up.
questions.explain: Dark windows, a creaking gate, and overgrown weeds suggest the house is scary, explaining Leo's caution.

questions.subject: reading
questions.passage: The desert is an arid place. Less than 10 inches of rain falls there each year, so plants and animals must find clever ways to survive without much water.
questions.prompt: What does the word 'arid' mean in this passage?
questions.explain: The clue 'less than 10 inches of rain' and 'without much water' tell us that arid means very dry.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Glass lets almost all light through. Wax paper lets some light through. Cardboard lets no light through. Plastic wrap lets almost all light through. Which two are MOST similar in how they handle light?
questions.explain: Glass and plastic wrap both let almost all light through, making them most similar in this property.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: What is 3/4 + 2/3?
questions.explain: Common denominator of 12: 9/12 + 8/12 = 17/12.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Maria ran 5/6 of a mile in the morning and 3/4 of a mile in the afternoon. How many miles did she run in all?
questions.explain: Common denominator of 12: 10/12 + 9/12 = 19/12 = 1 7/12 miles.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: What is 7/8 − 1/6?
questions.explain: Common denominator of 24: 21/24 − 4/24 = 17/24.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: What is 2/3 × 3/4?

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A recipe needs 3/4 cup of sugar for one batch of cookies. How much sugar is needed for 6 batches?

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: What is 1/2 ÷ 3?
questions.explain: Dividing by 3 = multiplying by 1/3: 1/2 × 1/3 = 1/6.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A 3-meter rope is cut into pieces that are each 1/4 meter long. How many pieces will there be?

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A swimming pool is 25 yards long. How many feet long is the pool?
questions.explain: 3 feet per yard: 25 × 3 = 75 feet.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A science experiment requires 2.5 liters of water. How many milliliters is that?

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A sprinter's time was 4.587 seconds. What is this rounded to the nearest hundredth?
questions.explain: The hundredths digit is 8; thousandths digit is 7 (≥5), so round up: 4.59.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: What does the adage 'A stitch in time saves nine' most likely mean?
options: Sewing is an important skill to learn early.
Fixing a small problem now prevents a much bigger problem later.
Nine stitches are always better than one.
It is better to wait and do things perfectly the first time.
questions.explain: The adage means that addressing a problem promptly keeps it from growing into a larger one.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which situation best illustrates the proverb 'Don't judge a book by its cover'?
options: Dani chose a library book with a colorful illustration on the front.
Marcus assumed a quiet new student was unfriendly, but they became best friends.
Priya read the back-cover summary before buying a book.
Leon returned a book because its cover was torn.
questions.explain: The proverb warns against judging based on outward appearance — as Marcus did with the new student.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: 'After studying all week, the final exam was a piece of cake for Jaylen.' What does 'a piece of cake' mean here?
options: The exam had a question about baking.
The exam was very long and difficult.
Jaylen was rewarded with dessert after the exam.
questions.explain: 'A piece of cake' is an idiom meaning something is very easy to do.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which sentence uses verb tense correctly and consistently?
options: Yesterday, Amara walks to the park and played on the swings.
Yesterday, Amara walked to the park and played on the swings.
Yesterday, Amara walked to the park and plays on the swings.
Yesterday, Amara will walk to the park and played on the swings.
questions.explain: 'Yesterday' signals past tense — both verbs must be past: walked and played.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: 'Her meticulous notes helped her identify the error.' What does 'meticulous' most likely mean?
questions.explain: Context shows her detailed notes helped find a mistake — meticulous means extremely careful and precise.

questions.subject: reading
questions.passage: Sofia set her alarm for 6:00 a.m., laid out her uniform the night before, and checked the weather app twice before bed. In the morning, she ate quickly, grabbed her shin guards from under her bed, and sprinted out the door before her mother finished calling her name.
questions.prompt: What can the reader most likely infer about Sofia?
options: Sofia was nervous about taking a test at school.
Sofia was excited and eager not to be late for a sports event.
Sofia was upset because she had forgotten something important.
Sofia did not want to go to school that morning.
questions.explain: The uniform, shin guards, and urgency all suggest Sofia was excited and rushing to a sports game or practice.

questions.subject: reading
questions.passage: Every day, Kenji practiced his violin even when his fingers ached and his friends were outside playing. His teacher told him he had no natural talent. But Kenji kept playing, kept practicing, kept failing — and kept starting again. Two years later, he performed a solo at the city concert hall to a standing ovation.
questions.prompt: What is the best statement of the theme of this passage?
options: Natural talent is the most important factor in success.
Persistent effort over time can lead to great achievement despite early setbacks.
It is important to listen to the advice of teachers and experts.
Playing an instrument is more valuable than spending time with friends.
questions.explain: Kenji's story shows that dedication and perseverance — not innate talent — led to his success.

questions.subject: reading
questions.passage: Plastic straws may seem harmless, but over 500 million are used and discarded every day in the United States alone. Many end up in oceans where sea turtles and seabirds mistake them for food. Switching to reusable or paper straws is a simple step every person can take to protect marine life and reduce plastic pollution.
questions.prompt: What is the author's primary purpose in writing this passage?
options: To entertain the reader with a story about ocean animals.
To explain how plastic straws are manufactured.
To persuade readers to stop using plastic straws.
To compare different types of reusable straws.
questions.explain: The author presents a problem and ends with a call to action — the purpose is to persuade readers to change behavior.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which set of inputs and outputs correctly describes photosynthesis?
options: Inputs: oxygen and water → Outputs: carbon dioxide and glucose
Inputs: carbon dioxide and water → Outputs: glucose and oxygen
Inputs: glucose and sunlight → Outputs: carbon dioxide and water
Inputs: nitrogen and sunlight → Outputs: glucose and carbon dioxide
questions.explain: Plants take in carbon dioxide and water and use sunlight to produce glucose and release oxygen.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A puddle disappears within hours on a sunny day even though no one poured it away. Which process and cause best explains this?
options: Condensation; the water cooled and turned into clouds.
Precipitation; the water fell back into the ground.
Evaporation; heat energy from the sun changed liquid water into water vapor.
Transpiration; surrounding plants absorbed the water through their roots.
questions.explain: Evaporation occurs when solar heat causes liquid water to change into water vapor and rise into the atmosphere.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Count the apples: 🍎🍎🍎🍎🍎🍎🍎🍎🍎🍎🍎🍎🍎. How many?
questions.explain: Count one by one: 1, 2, 3 ... 13. There are 13 apples!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Count BACKWARDS. What comes after 10, 9, 8, 7...?
questions.explain: When counting backwards: 10, 9, 8, 7, 6. Six comes next!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: What number comes RIGHT AFTER 17?
questions.explain: When counting forward, 18 comes right after 17!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: What number comes RIGHT BEFORE 12?
questions.explain: 11 comes right before 12 when counting forward!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Which group has MORE? ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ OR 🌙🌙🌙🌙🌙
questions.explain: 7 stars vs 5 moons — 7 is MORE than 5!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Leo, Mia, and Sam are in line. Mia is THIRD. Who is in front of her?
questions.explain: Third means two friends are in front: Leo (1st) and Sam (2nd) come before Mia.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Count by TENS: 10, 20, 30, ___
questions.explain: When counting by 10s, add 10 each time: 10, 20, 30, 40!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Count by TWOS: 2, 4, 6, ___
questions.explain: Count by 2s — skip a number each time: 2, 4, 6, 8!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: What does the number ZERO mean?
questions.explain: Zero means nothing at all — like having 0 cookies means you have none!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: What number is 1 MORE than 8?
questions.explain: One more than 8 is 9. Think: 8, then one more jump → 9!

questions.subject: math
questions.explain: 4 frogs plus 3 more frogs = 7 frogs total. Count on: 4 ... 5, 6, 7!

questions.subject: math
questions.explain: Start with 9 stars, cross out 4 — 5 stars are left!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Ana baked 3 🍪 and then baked 4 more. How many cookies does she have now?
questions.explain: 3 cookies plus 4 more = 7 cookies. You can count all: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: 6 🐦 sat on a branch. 2 flew away. How many are left?
questions.explain: 6 birds minus 2 birds = 4 birds left on the branch!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: 10 = 7 + ?  What number is missing?
questions.explain: 7 + 3 = 10. If you have 7, you need 3 more to reach 10!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Which two numbers make 5?
questions.explain: 2 + 3 = 5. You can check: 1+3=4, 2+4=6, 3+3=6. Only 2+3 makes 5!

questions.subject: math
questions.explain: Zero means nothing. Adding nothing to 9 keeps it 9. 0 + 9 = 9!

questions.subject: math
questions.explain: Start at 10, take away 6: 10, 9, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4. You land on 4!

questions.subject: math
questions.explain: 12 is one group of ten and 2 ones: 10 + 2 = 12!

questions.subject: math
questions.explain: 17 is one ten and 7 ones: 10 + 7 = 17!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Which number has 1 TEN and 5 ONES?
questions.explain: 1 ten = 10, plus 5 ones = 15! The number is 15.

questions.subject: math
questions.explain: 19 is one ten and 9 ones: 10 + 9 = 19!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Pencil A is 6 cubes long. Pencil B is 4 cubes long. Which pencil is LONGER?
questions.explain: 6 cubes is longer than 4 cubes. Pencil A is longer!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Which weighs MORE?
questions.explain: An elephant weighs much more than an apple — thousands of pounds more!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Which container holds MORE water?
questions.explain: A bucket is much bigger than a teaspoon — it holds a lot more water!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Sort these by SIZE from smallest to biggest: 🐜 🐕 🐘
questions.explain: Smallest to biggest: ant 🐜, then dog 🐕, then elephant 🐘!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Which shape is a 3D SOLID you can hold in your hand?
questions.explain: A sphere is a 3D solid — like a ball you can hold! A circle is just a flat drawing.

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: The 🐦 bird is flying directly OVER the branch. Where is the bird?
questions.explain: The bird is flying ABOVE the branch — above means higher up, over the top!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Which shape is FLAT (2D — only has length and width)?
questions.explain: A circle is flat — you can draw it on paper. Cones, cubes, and spheres are 3D solids!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: How many CORNERS does a rectangle have?
questions.explain: A rectangle has 4 corners — one at each point where two sides meet!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: What sound does 🦊 FOX start with?
questions.explain: FOX starts with the /f/ sound. F says /f/ — like Frog, Fish, and Fox!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: What sound does 🏠 HOUSE start with?
questions.explain: HOUSE starts with /h/. H says /h/ — like Hat, Hand, and House!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: What sound does the word MAP end with?
questions.explain: M-A-P. The last letter is P and it says /p/. Map ends with /p/!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Put these sounds together: /p/ ... /i/ ... /g/. What word do they make?
questions.explain: /p/ + /i/ + /g/ = PIG! The sounds blend together to make the word pig!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Which picture name starts with the short /ĕ/ sound, like in EGG?
questions.explain: EGG starts with the short /ĕ/ sound — like the 'e' in egg, end, and elephant!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Which word begins with the /w/ sound?
questions.explain: WIG begins with /w/! W says /w/ — like Water, Wind, and Wig!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Which word RHYMES with MOP?
questions.explain: MOP and TOP rhyme — they both end with the -OP sound!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Which group of words belongs to the -IG word family?
questions.explain: Big, dig, and fig all end in -IG. They are in the -ig word family!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Fill in the blank: '___ went to the park.' Which word fits?
questions.explain: THEY is the correct sight word: 'They went to the park.' THEY means a group of people!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Fill in the blank: 'She ___ hello to me.'
questions.explain: SAID is the past tense sight word: 'She said hello.' Said means she already spoke!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Fill in the blank: 'Please ___ to my birthday party!'
questions.explain: COME is the sight word: 'Please come to my party!' Come means to go to a place!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Fill in the blank: 'We ___ to the park after school.'
questions.explain: WENT is the past tense sight word: 'We went to the park.' Went means we already went there!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Fill in the blank: 'I ___ a pet goldfish.'
questions.explain: HAVE is the correct sight word: 'I have a pet goldfish.' Have means you own or possess something!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Fill in the blank: 'It ___ a sunny day at the beach.'
questions.explain: WAS is the past tense sight word: 'It was a sunny day.' Was means it happened in the past!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: A story is about a girl named Zoe who finds a lost puppy and brings it home. What is the story MOSTLY about?
options: A girl who finds a lost puppy
questions.explain: The story is mostly about Zoe finding a lost puppy — that is the main idea!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: In a story: First Mia woke up. Next she ate breakfast. Then she went to school. What happened FIRST?
questions.explain: FIRST, Mia woke up! The word 'first' in the story tells us what happened at the very beginning.

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: In a story, a rabbit named Benny hops through the forest. Benny is a ___?
questions.explain: Benny the rabbit is a CHARACTER — a character is a person or animal in a story!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: A story says: 'The children played in the barn with the horses and chickens.' Where does the story take place?
questions.explain: The story takes place at a barn on a farm — that's the SETTING, where the story happens!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: In a story, dark clouds fill the sky and thunder rumbles. What might happen NEXT?
questions.explain: Dark clouds and thunder usually mean rain is coming! You used clues to make a prediction!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: A book shows real photos of tigers and explains where tigers really live. Is this book FICTION or NONFICTION?
questions.explain: Nonfiction uses real facts and real photos. A book about real tigers is nonfiction!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Where on a book do you find the TITLE?
questions.explain: The title — the name of the book — is on the FRONT COVER! It tells you what the book is about.

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: In English, we start reading on which side of the page?
questions.explain: We always start reading on the LEFT side and move to the right — just like how our eyes move!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: 'I love pizza!' How many WORDS are in this sentence?
questions.explain: Count the words: I (1) love (2) pizza (3) — there are 3 words!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: 'My dog is friendly' — what PUNCTUATION mark goes at the end of this telling sentence?
questions.explain: A telling sentence ends with a PERIOD (.). 'My dog is friendly.' is a statement, so it gets a period!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: The word 'sun' in UPPERCASE letters is ___?
questions.explain: Uppercase means ALL capital letters. 'sun' in uppercase is SUN — every letter is a capital!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: How many SYLLABLES (beats) are in the word RAINBOW?
questions.explain: RAIN-BOW has 2 syllables. Clap with me: RAIN (clap) BOW (clap) — 2 claps, 2 syllables!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: What is the FIRST sound in the word MOON?
questions.explain: MOON starts with the /m/ sound. M says /m/ — like Moose, Milk, and Moon!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Put the sounds together: /f/ ... /u/ ... /n/. What word do they make?
questions.explain: /f/ + /u/ + /n/ = FUN! Blend the sounds together and you get the word fun!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: How many SOUNDS are in the word CHOP?
questions.explain: CHOP has 3 sounds: /ch/ + /o/ + /p/. Even though 'ch' is two letters, it makes ONE sound!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: The word is CAP. Change the /k/ sound to /m/. What is the NEW word?
questions.explain: CAP with /m/ instead of /k/ = MAP! /m/ + /a/ + /p/ = map!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Does CAKE rhyme with LAKE?
questions.explain: Yes! CAKE and LAKE both end with the -AKE sound. They rhyme!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Add /pl/ to the beginning of ANT. What new word do you get?
questions.explain: /pl/ + ANT = PLANT! You added /pl/ to the front of ant to make plant!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Say FARM. Now say it again WITHOUT the /f/ sound at the beginning. What's left?
questions.explain: FARM without /f/ = ARM! Take /f/ away from farm and you're left with arm!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which letter shows the UPPERCASE 'j'?
questions.explain: J is the uppercase (capital) version of the letter j. Capital letters are bigger!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which shows the LOWERCASE letter 'r'?
questions.explain: r is the lowercase (small) version of the letter R!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: What letter comes AFTER Q in the alphabet?
questions.explain: In the alphabet, Q is followed by R: ...O, P, Q, R, S, T... R comes after Q!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which lowercase letter matches the uppercase 'F'?
questions.explain: The lowercase of F is f. F and f are the same letter — just different sizes!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: 'b' and 'd' look alike! Which letter says /b/ like the word BALL?
questions.explain: b says /b/ like BALL and BED. The letter b has its bump facing right!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: A, E, I, O, U are called ___?
questions.explain: A, E, I, O, U are VOWELS! Every word needs at least one vowel. All other letters are consonants!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: A B C D __ F — What is the MISSING letter?
questions.explain: The alphabet goes A, B, C, D, E, F — E is the missing letter!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What do PLANTS need to grow? 🌱
questions.explain: Plants need sunlight ☀️, water 💧, and soil 🌍 to grow — these are a plant's basic needs!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which of these do ANIMALS need to survive?
questions.explain: Animals need food to eat, water to drink, and shelter to stay safe — just like us!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which part of a plant soaks up water and food from the soil?
questions.explain: ROOTS grow underground and soak up water and nutrients from the soil for the plant!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Is a 🪨 rock LIVING or NON-LIVING?
options: Non-living — it doesn't grow or breathe
questions.explain: Rocks are NON-LIVING. They don't eat, breathe, grow, or have babies — living things do all of these!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A baby dog is called a ___?
questions.explain: A baby dog is called a PUPPY! Baby cat = kitten, baby cow = calf, baby horse = foal!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What type of weather brings thunder ⚡ and lightning?
questions.explain: A THUNDERSTORM brings thunder, lightning, and heavy rain — it can be very loud!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: It is snowing outside ❄️. What should you wear?
questions.explain: When it snows, you wear a warm winter coat, hat, gloves, and boots to stay warm!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: When the ☀️ sun goes down in the evening, it becomes ___?
questions.explain: When the sun sets (goes down), it gets dark and becomes NIGHTTIME!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The ☀️ sun gives Earth two important things. What are they?
questions.explain: The sun gives us LIGHT to see during the day and WARMTH (heat) to keep Earth from freezing!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: When water droplets in clouds get heavy enough, they fall to Earth as ___?
questions.explain: When clouds get heavy with water, droplets fall as RAIN or, in cold weather, as SNOW!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: To open a door by pulling the handle toward you, you ___?
questions.explain: You PULL the door handle toward you to open it. Pull means to bring something closer to you!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Does a 🍋 lemon SINK or FLOAT in water?
options: Sink — it goes to the bottom
questions.explain: A lemon FLOATS! Its air pockets and skin make it light enough to stay on top of water!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which is HOT?
questions.explain: Fire 🔥 is HOT! Ice is cold. Fire gives off heat that you can feel — always be safe around fire!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which material is HARD?
questions.explain: WOOD is hard — you can't squish it! Cotton is soft and fluffy. Hard materials don't change shape when you push them.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Shadows are made when an object BLOCKS ___?
questions.explain: Shadows form when an object blocks LIGHT — the object stops the light from passing through, making a dark shape!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: There is a fire in a building! Who do you call for help? 🚒
questions.explain: Call FIREFIGHTERS when there is a fire! They rush to help in their big red fire trucks 🚒!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: Your teacher is explaining something. What is the BEST thing to do?
questions.explain: When the teacher is speaking, we LISTEN quietly and pay attention — that is respectful and helps us learn!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: New York City has 5 boroughs. Which one has the famous Coney Island beach?
questions.explain: Coney Island beach is in BROOKLYN — one of NYC's 5 boroughs! Brooklyn is also home to the Brooklyn Bridge 🌉!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: On most maps, NORTH is shown at the ___?
questions.explain: On most maps, NORTH is at the TOP! The compass rose shows N (North) pointing up!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: We celebrate Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Day in which month?
questions.explain: MLK Day is in JANUARY — the third Monday of January. We honor Dr. King's work for equality and peace!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: 'Treat others the way YOU want to be treated.' This is called the ___?
questions.explain: This is the GOLDEN RULE! If you want to be treated kindly, treat others kindly too!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: How many STRIPES does the American flag have?
questions.explain: The American flag has 13 stripes — 7 red and 6 white. They stand for the original 13 colonies!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: Every student has the RIGHT to ___?
questions.explain: Every student has the right to LEARN in a safe school — that's an important right for all children!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: Taking TURNS and making sure everyone gets a chance is called being ___?
questions.explain: Taking turns and sharing means being FAIR — fairness means everyone gets an equal chance!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: Families can be ___?
questions.explain: Families come in MANY different types — some have two parents, one parent, grandparents, or other relatives. All families are special!

questions.subject: logic
questions.prompt: Which one does NOT belong? 🐶 🐱 🐟 🍎
questions.explain: The apple 🍎 does NOT belong — the dog, cat, and fish are all ANIMALS. An apple is a fruit, not an animal!

questions.subject: logic
questions.prompt: What comes NEXT? 🔴🔵🔴🔵🔴___
questions.explain: The pattern is red, blue, red, blue, red... so BLUE comes next! It's an AB pattern!

questions.subject: logic
questions.prompt: You left your umbrella at home. Now you are soaking WET from the rain. What CAUSED you to get wet?
options: It rained and you had no umbrella
questions.explain: It rained AND you had no umbrella — that's the CAUSE! The effect was getting wet!

questions.subject: logic
questions.prompt: A WHEEL is a part of a ___?
questions.explain: Wheels are parts of vehicles like cars 🚗 and bicycles 🚲 — they spin to help things move!

questions.subject: logic
questions.prompt: If there are DARK CLOUDS ⛅ in the sky, you should probably bring a ___?
questions.explain: Dark clouds often mean RAIN is coming — an umbrella ☂️ will keep you dry!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Any number multiplied by ZERO always equals ___?
questions.explain: Any number × 0 = 0. Zero groups of anything is still nothing!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: 24 students need to be divided into 4 equal groups. How many students in each group?
questions.explain: 24 ÷ 4 = 6. Divide 24 into 4 equal groups: 6 students in each group!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: 7 × ___ = 35. What is the missing number?
questions.explain: 7 × 5 = 35. Think: 7 groups of 5, or count by 7s until you reach 35!

questions.subject: math
questions.explain: 6 × 4 = 4 × 6 = 24. The commutative property says you can switch the order of factors!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Use the distributive property: 3 × (2 + 4) = ?

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Tyrell has 4 bags with 7 apples each. He gives 8 apples to his class. How many does he have left?
questions.explain: Step 1: 4 × 7 = 28 apples total. Step 2: 28 - 8 = 20. Tyrell has 20 apples left!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: 42 students sit in 6 equal rows. How many students are in each row?
questions.explain: 42 ÷ 6 = 7. Six equal rows with 7 students each: 6 × 7 = 42 ✓

questions.subject: math
questions.explain: 8 × 3 = 24, so 24 ÷ 8 = 3. Multiplication and division are fact families!

questions.subject: math
questions.explain: 6 × 9 = 54. You can remember: count by 6s nine times, or by 9s six times!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: 4 × 6 × 2 = ? (Hint: multiply any two numbers first)
questions.explain: You can multiply in any order: (4×6)×2 = 24×2 = 48, or 4×(6×2) = 4×12 = 48!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Round 47 to the nearest 10.
questions.explain: 47 is between 40 and 50. The digit in the ones place is 7 (≥5), so round UP to 50!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Round 352 to the nearest 100.
questions.explain: 352 is between 300 and 400. The tens digit is 5 (≥5), so round UP to 400!

questions.subject: math
questions.explain: 700 - 264: Borrow across the zeros — 700 → 6 hundreds, 9 tens, 10 ones. 10-4=6, 9-6=3, 6-2=4. Answer: 436!

questions.subject: math
questions.explain: 40 × 7 = (4 × 7) × 10 = 28 × 10 = 280. Multiplying by a multiple of 10 shifts the value one place!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: What is the VALUE of the digit 6 in the number 463?
questions.explain: In 463, the 6 is in the TENS place. Its value is 6 × 10 = 60!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: On a number line from 0 to 1, where is 3/4?
questions.explain: 3/4 is 3 out of 4 equal parts. It sits between 1/2 and 1 — closer to 1!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Which fraction is EQUAL to 2/4?
questions.explain: 2/4 = 1/2. Both show half of a whole. Divide top and bottom by 2: 2÷2 / 4÷2 = 1/2!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Which fraction is LARGER: 1/4 or 3/4?
questions.explain: 3/4 > 1/4. Same denominator, so compare numerators: 3 > 1, so 3/4 is larger!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Which fraction is LARGER: 1/3 or 1/5?
questions.explain: 1/3 > 1/5. Same numerator, so the SMALLER the denominator, the LARGER the piece. Thirds are bigger than fifths!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: There are 12 students in a group. 1/3 have dogs at home. How many students have dogs?
questions.explain: 1/3 of 12 = 12 ÷ 3 = 4. Four students have dogs!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A pizza is cut into 6 equal slices. What fraction does ONE slice represent?
questions.explain: 1 out of 6 equal slices = 1/6. The denominator (6) tells how many equal parts total!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A clock shows the hour hand just past 3 and the minute hand on 9. What time is it?
questions.explain: Minute hand on 9 = 45 minutes. Hour hand has passed 3. The time is 3:45!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A movie starts at 2:00 PM and lasts 1 hour and 30 minutes. When does it end?
questions.explain: 2:00 + 1 hour = 3:00, then + 30 minutes = 3:30 PM!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A library book weighs 500 grams. A backpack weighs 2 kilograms. Which weighs MORE?
questions.explain: 2 kilograms = 2,000 grams. 2,000 g > 500 g. The backpack weighs more!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A fish tank holds 80 liters. A water bottle holds 1 liter. How many FULL bottles fill the tank?
questions.explain: 80 liters ÷ 1 liter per bottle = 80 bottles. You'd need 80 full bottles!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A bar graph shows: Soccer 18, Basketball 12, Baseball 7, Swimming 10 votes. Which sport had the MOST votes?
questions.explain: Soccer had 18 votes — the highest bar on the graph. Soccer got the MOST votes!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: Which shape is a QUADRILATERAL (has exactly 4 sides)?
questions.explain: RECTANGLE is a quadrilateral — it has exactly 4 sides and 4 angles. Quad means four!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A rectangle is 6 cm long and 4 cm wide. What is its PERIMETER?
questions.explain: Perimeter = 6+4+6+4 = 20 cm. Add all four sides: two lengths (6) and two widths (4)!

questions.subject: math
questions.prompt: A circle is divided into 8 equal parts. What fraction does each part represent?
questions.explain: 1 part out of 8 equal parts = 1/8. Each slice is one-eighth of the whole circle!

questions.subject: reading
questions.passage: Bees are amazing insects. They pollinate flowers by carrying pollen from plant to plant. Without bees, many fruits and vegetables would not grow. Bees also make honey, which people have eaten for thousands of years. A single hive can hold 60,000 bees!
questions.prompt: What is the MAIN IDEA of this passage?
options: Bees are important insects that help plants and make honey
questions.explain: The passage mostly discusses how bees help plants (pollination) and make honey — they are important and amazing!

questions.subject: reading
questions.passage: Sea turtles travel thousands of miles across the ocean. They return to the exact beach where they were born to lay their eggs. Scientists believe sea turtles use Earth's magnetic field like a compass to navigate.
questions.prompt: Based on the text, what do sea turtles use to find their way?
questions.explain: The text says: 'scientists believe sea turtles use Earth's magnetic field like a compass to navigate.' That's your text evidence!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Maya studied all week for her big math test. When she got her paper back, she jumped out of her seat and shouted 'YES!' What can you infer about Maya's grade?
options: She forgot to put her name on the paper
She probably got a great grade
questions.explain: You can INFER Maya did well — she studied hard and her reaction (jumping, shouting 'YES!') shows excitement and happiness!

questions.subject: reading
questions.passage: Eating breakfast gives your brain energy to focus at school. Studies show students who eat breakfast score higher on tests and feel happier throughout the day. Start your morning with a healthy breakfast!
questions.prompt: What is the AUTHOR'S PURPOSE in writing this?
options: To entertain with a funny story
To persuade you to eat breakfast
To describe what breakfast looks like
To explain the history of breakfast
questions.explain: The author wants to PERSUADE (convince) you to eat breakfast by giving reasons why it helps. The last sentence is a call to action!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: Both dogs and cats are popular pets. Dogs are loyal and love to play outside. Cats are independent and prefer staying indoors. Both need food, water, and love from their owners. How are dogs and cats ALIKE?
options: Both need food, water, and love
questions.explain: The passage says BOTH pets need food, water, and love — that's what they have in common!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: It rained heavily for three days without stopping. The river near town rose higher and higher. Soon the water spilled over its banks and flooded the streets. What CAUSED the flooding?
options: People forgot to close a dam
questions.explain: Three days of heavy rain caused the river to overflow — the CAUSE is the heavy rain, the EFFECT is the flooding!

questions.subject: reading
questions.passage: Every time Kezia tried to ride her skateboard, she fell down. Her knees hurt and she felt embarrassed. But she kept trying, day after day. After two weeks, she was zooming down the sidewalk. 'I never would have learned if I gave up,' she told her mom.
questions.prompt: What is the THEME (lesson) of this story?
options: Persistence pays off — keep trying even when it's hard
questions.explain: Kezia kept trying even after falling, and she succeeded. The theme is: don't give up — persistence leads to success!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: In a story: Marcus packed his backpack. He ate breakfast. He walked to the bus stop. Then the bus arrived. What happened SECOND?
options: He walked to the bus stop
questions.explain: The sequence is: pack backpack → eat breakfast → walk to stop → bus arrives. Second is eating breakfast!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: A story begins: 'I grabbed my lunch and ran out the door. I didn't want to be late!' What point of view is this story written in?
questions.explain: Using 'I' means FIRST PERSON point of view — the narrator is telling their own story!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: In a nonfiction book, bold headings like 'Chapter 3: The Water Cycle' help readers ___?
options: Know how long the book is
questions.explain: Headings help you FIND topics and preview what each section is about — they're like a road map for the text!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: The word BARK can mean the outer covering of a tree OR the sound a dog makes. This is called a ___?
questions.explain: BARK is a multiple-meaning word — same spelling, different meanings. Context clues tell you which meaning to use!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: The prefix UN- means 'not.' What does UNHAPPY mean?
questions.explain: UN- means 'not,' so UNHAPPY means NOT happy. Other examples: undo, unfair, unlike!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: The prefix RE- means 'again.' What does REWRITE mean?
questions.explain: RE- means 'again,' so REWRITE means to write again. Rebuild = build again, replay = play again!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: The suffix -FUL means 'full of.' What does CHEERFUL mean?
questions.explain: -FUL means 'full of.' CHEERFUL means full of cheer (happiness). Helpful = full of help, colorful = full of color!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: The suffix -LESS means 'without.' What does HOPELESS mean?
questions.explain: -LESS means 'without.' HOPELESS means without hope. Careless = without care, speechless = without speech!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: What is the ANTONYM (opposite) of ANCIENT?
questions.explain: ANCIENT means very old. Its antonym (opposite) is MODERN or NEW. Ancient ruins vs. modern buildings!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: What is a SYNONYM (same meaning) for EXHAUSTED?
questions.explain: EXHAUSTED means completely worn out — very tired. Tired, worn out, and weary are all synonyms for exhausted!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: 'By noon, the hikers were absolutely FAMISHED and stopped to eat lunch.' What does FAMISHED most likely mean?
questions.explain: Context clue: they 'stopped to eat lunch' because they were famished — FAMISHED means very hungry!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: 'After recess, the classroom was a ZOO.' What does this METAPHOR mean?
options: Wild animals were in the classroom
The classroom was very noisy and out of control
questions.explain: This METAPHOR compares the classroom to a zoo — not literally, but to say it was chaotic and noisy, like animals!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: 'Marcus was as brave as a lion.' What type of figurative language is this?
questions.explain: A SIMILE compares two things using 'as' or 'like.' 'As brave AS a lion' — comparing Marcus to a lion using 'as'!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: 'Sally sells seashells by the seashore.' What literary device repeats the same beginning consonant sound?
questions.explain: ALLITERATION repeats the same consonant sound at the start of nearby words. Here, the /s/ sound repeats: Sally, sells, seashells, seashore!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: 'The old oak tree whispered secrets to the wind.' What literary device gives human qualities to the tree?
questions.explain: PERSONIFICATION gives human qualities (like whispering) to non-human things. Trees can't really whisper — that's a human action!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: In the sentence 'The bees BUZZED loudly around the hive,' BUZZED is an example of ___?
questions.explain: ONOMATOPOEIA is when a word sounds like what it describes. BUZZ sounds like the sound bees actually make!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: 'I have told you a MILLION times to clean your room!' This is an example of ___?
questions.explain: HYPERBOLE is a huge exaggeration. You definitely haven't said it a million times — it's used to express frustration with emphasis!

questions.subject: reading
questions.prompt: A book features made-up characters going on imaginary adventures. This book is ___?
questions.explain: FICTION means made-up stories with imaginary characters and events. Nonfiction is about real people and real events!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: What is the PLURAL of MOUSE?
questions.explain: MOUSE → MICE is an irregular plural — you don't just add -s! Other irregular plurals: foot→feet, tooth→teeth!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: What is the PLURAL of CHILD?
questions.explain: CHILD → CHILDREN is an irregular plural. Not 'childs' — English has many irregular plurals to memorize!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Yesterday, I ___ to the grocery store. (past tense)
questions.explain: WENT is the past tense of 'go' — an irregular verb. 'Yesterday I went to the store' is correct!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: The sunset was ___. (Choose the ADJECTIVE — a word that describes the noun 'sunset')
questions.explain: BEAUTIFUL is an adjective — it describes the noun 'sunset.' Beautifully is an adverb (used to describe verbs)!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: She ran ___. (Choose the ADVERB — a word that describes HOW she ran)
questions.explain: QUICKLY is an adverb ending in -ly. Adverbs describe verbs. She ran QUICKLY tells us HOW she ran!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: 'The dogs ___ happy to see their owner.' Which verb is correct?
questions.explain: 'Dogs' is plural, so use 'are': 'The dogs ARE happy.' Singular uses 'is'; plural uses 'are'!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: 'Omar brought ___ lunch to school.' Choose the correct pronoun for Omar (one boy).
questions.explain: Omar is one boy, so use HIS. 'Omar brought HIS lunch to school.' Her = girl, their = group, his = one boy!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Combine into ONE compound sentence: 'I love soccer.' + 'My brother prefers basketball.'
options: I love soccer, but my brother prefers basketball.
I love soccer. My brother prefers basketball.
I love soccer my brother prefers basketball.
questions.explain: A compound sentence joins two ideas with a comma + conjunction (but, and, or). Use a comma before 'but'!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: 'The ___ collar is red.' Add a possessive noun to show the collar belongs to the dog.
questions.explain: DOG'S (with apostrophe + s) shows possession: 'The dog's collar is red.' The collar belongs to the dog!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which sentence uses CAPITALIZATION correctly?
questions.explain: Names of specific pets are proper nouns and need capital letters. Rex is the pet's name, so it's capitalized. Only the first word and proper noun get capitals!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which sentence uses COMMAS correctly in a list?
options: I need apples oranges and bananas.
I need apples, oranges, and bananas.
I need, apples, oranges, and bananas.
I need apples, oranges and, bananas.
questions.explain: In a list of 3+ items, use commas to separate them: apples, oranges, and bananas. The comma before 'and' is correct!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses QUOTATION MARKS for spoken words?
questions.explain: Quotation marks go around the EXACT words someone speaks. The comma goes before the opening quote, and the period goes inside the closing quote!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: 'Don't' is a contraction. What two words does it combine?
questions.explain: DON'T = DO + NOT. The apostrophe replaces the 'o' in 'not.' Other contractions: can't=cannot, won't=will not!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: 'What an amazing sunset this is!' What type of sentence is this?
questions.explain: An EXCLAMATORY sentence shows strong emotion and ends with an exclamation mark (!). 'What a...!' and 'How...!' are exclamatory patterns!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: The FIRST sentence of a paragraph that tells what the paragraph is about is called the ___?
questions.explain: The TOPIC SENTENCE introduces the main idea of a paragraph — it tells the reader what they're about to read!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: After the topic sentence, you should add ___?
questions.explain: After the topic sentence, add SUPPORTING DETAILS — facts, examples, and explanations that prove your main idea!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: The LAST sentence of a paragraph that wraps up the main idea is called the ___?
questions.explain: The CONCLUDING SENTENCE ends the paragraph by restating or summarizing the main idea — it wraps everything up!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: 'First... Next... Finally...' — these connecting words are called ___?
questions.explain: TRANSITIONAL WORDS (like first, next, then, finally) help readers follow the order of events or steps in writing!

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Which sentence is a RUN-ON that needs to be fixed?
options: We went to the park it was very sunny and fun I played all day.
questions.explain: A RUN-ON joins too many ideas without proper punctuation. Fix it: 'We went to the park. It was sunny and fun. I played all day.'

questions.subject: literacy
questions.prompt: Instead of writing 'The elephant was very big,' a more vivid word choice would be ___?
options: The elephant was very, very big.
questions.explain: ENORMOUS is more precise and vivid than 'very big.' Strong writers choose specific, descriptive words instead of 'very + adjective'!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A HABITAT is ___?
options: The natural home and environment where an organism lives
questions.explain: A HABITAT is the natural environment where a living thing makes its home — like a pond for frogs or a forest for deer!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: In a food chain, plants are called PRODUCERS because they ___?
options: Make their own food using sunlight
questions.explain: Plants are PRODUCERS — they make (produce) their own food using sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide through photosynthesis!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A child has the same eye color as her mother. This is an example of ___?
questions.explain: Eye color is an INHERITED TRAIT — a characteristic passed from parents to children through genes!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is the correct ORDER of a butterfly's life cycle?
questions.explain: Butterfly life cycle: Egg → Larva (caterpillar) → Pupa (chrysalis) → Adult (butterfly). This is called METAMORPHOSIS!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A polar bear has thick white fur in an Arctic environment. This is an example of ___?
questions.explain: Thick fur to survive cold, and white coloring for camouflage, are ADAPTATIONS — features that help organisms survive in their environment!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Coal and oil took millions of years to form underground. They are examples of ___?
options: Nonrenewable resources (take too long to replace)
questions.explain: Coal and oil are NONRENEWABLE — once used, they can't be replaced in a human lifetime. Wind and solar are renewable!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Wind and water slowly wearing away rocks and soil over time is called ___?
questions.explain: EROSION is the gradual wearing away of rock and soil by wind, water, or ice. The Grand Canyon was carved by erosion!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: In the water cycle, water vapor in the air cools and forms clouds. This step is called ___?
questions.explain: CONDENSATION is when water vapor (gas) cools and turns back into liquid water droplets, forming clouds!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which type of rock forms when melted rock (lava or magma) cools and hardens?
questions.explain: IGNEOUS rock forms from cooling magma or lava. Obsidian and basalt are examples of igneous rocks!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A DESERT climate is usually described as ___?
questions.explain: Deserts are HOT and DRY — they get very little rain (less than 25 cm per year). The Sahara is the world's largest hot desert!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Water can exist as liquid water, solid ice, and ___?
questions.explain: Water exists in three STATES OF MATTER: liquid (water), solid (ice), and gas (steam / water vapor)!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A magnet ATTRACTS (pulls toward) objects made of ___?
questions.explain: Magnets attract IRON and STEEL (and nickel and cobalt). Wood, plastic, and glass are not magnetic!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: When you block light with your hand, you create a ___?
questions.explain: A SHADOW forms when an opaque object blocks light — light cannot pass through, so a dark area appears on the other side!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Sound is produced by ___?
options: Vibrations that travel through the air
questions.explain: Sound is produced by VIBRATIONS — when objects vibrate (shake), they create sound waves that travel through air to our ears!

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: When you slide a book across a table, FRICTION between the book and table causes the book to ___?
questions.explain: FRICTION is a force that opposes motion — it slows moving objects down. Rough surfaces create more friction than smooth ones!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: New York City is made up of how many BOROUGHS?
questions.explain: NYC has 5 BOROUGHS: Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, The Bronx, and Staten Island — each is like its own city within NYC!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: The colonists' fight for independence from British rule in the 1700s is called ___?
questions.explain: The AMERICAN REVOLUTION (1775–1783) was when the 13 colonies fought for independence from Britain. The Declaration of Independence was signed in 1776!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: Millions of people came to America through Ellis Island in New York Harbor. These people are called ___?
questions.explain: IMMIGRANTS are people who come from another country to live in a new country. Ellis Island was the gateway for millions between 1892 and 1954!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: The LENAPE were the original people of the New York area. They are examples of ___?
questions.explain: The LENAPE are NATIVE AMERICANS (Indigenous people) — they lived in the New York area long before European explorers arrived!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: The Brooklyn Bridge connects Brooklyn to ___?
questions.explain: The Brooklyn Bridge connects BROOKLYN to MANHATTAN across the East River. It opened in 1883 and is still used every day!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: On a map, N, S, E, and W stand for the four CARDINAL DIRECTIONS. What does N stand for?
questions.explain: N = NORTH, S = South, E = East, W = West. These are the four cardinal (main) directions!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: The southeastern United States (like Florida and Georgia) is known for its ___?
questions.explain: The Southeast US has a WARM, HUMID climate — hot summers, mild winters, and lots of rainfall because of its location near the Gulf of Mexico!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: The United States government has THREE branches. Which branch MAKES the laws?
questions.explain: CONGRESS (Senate + House of Representatives) is the Legislative Branch — it MAKES the laws. The President signs them, the Supreme Court interprets them!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: A farmer grows corn and sells it at a market. The corn is an example of ___?
questions.explain: GOODS are physical things you can buy and use — like corn, cars, or clothes. SERVICES are actions people do for you — like haircuts or doctor visits!

questions.subject: social_studies
questions.prompt: Who was the FIRST President of the United States?
questions.explain: GEORGE WASHINGTON was the first President of the United States, serving from 1789 to 1797. He led the Continental Army during the Revolution!
